text: SUCCESS STORY — Sarah Namugga, 24, Poultry Farmer, Mukono: Sarah started with just 50 broiler chicks and UGX 800,000 borrowed from her village savings group (VSLA) in 2024. Her first batch was devastated by Newcastle disease because she didn't vaccinate properly — she lost 30 birds and nearly gave up. 'I cried for two days, then I went to the district veterinary officer and learned everything about vaccination schedules.' Her second batch of 100 birds survived at 95%. She now runs 3 batches of 200 birds each, supplying 4 restaurants in Mukono. Monthly profit: UGX 1,200,000. Her advice: 'Start small, learn from every mistake, and never skip vaccinations. The birds don't forgive shortcuts.'

SUCCESS STORY — Brian Ochieng, 22, Rolex Vendor to Caterer, Kampala: Brian started selling rolexes near Makerere University gate in 2023 with UGX 150,000. He sold 30-40 rolexes daily. 'I noticed students wanted more variety, so I added samosas, mandazi, and fresh juice.' His turning point came when a lecturer asked him to cater a department meeting. He earned UGX 250,000 for one event. 'I realized catering pays 5x more than street vending.' He now does 3-4 catering events weekly while keeping his rolex stand (run by his trained assistant). Monthly income: UGX 2,000,000+. His advice: 'Keep your first business running while you grow. The rolex stand feeds my family while catering builds my future.'

SUCCESS STORY — Grace Atuhaire, 26, Tailor, Mbarara: Grace learned tailoring from her aunt and started with a second-hand sewing machine worth UGX 350,000. She struggled for 6 months with few customers. 'I was making maybe UGX 5,000 a day. I almost went back to selling tomatoes.' Her breakthrough: she walked into 5 primary schools and offered to make sample uniforms for free. Three schools gave her contracts. She hired 2 apprentices and now produces 500+ uniforms per term. She also makes kitenge dresses for weddings. Monthly income: UGX 1,800,000 in peak season, UGX 600,000 in low season. Her advice: 'Don't wait for customers to find you. Go find them. And always deliver on time — your reputation is everything.'

SUCCESS STORY — Moses Ssempijja, 28, Mobile Money + Boda Repair, Kawempe: Moses combined two businesses that feed each other. He runs a mobile money kiosk at a boda stage, and repairs motorcycles in the space behind it. 'Boda riders come to withdraw money and I fix their bikes. Or they come for repairs and I help them send money.' He started with UGX 1,500,000 total. His mobile money commission: UGX 400,000/month. Repair income: UGX 800,000/month. 'The two businesses together earn more than either alone because customers come for one and use the other.' His advice: 'Think about businesses that complement each other. And always keep learning — I watch YouTube videos about motorcycle repair every night.'

SUCCESS STORY — Anita Nakirya, 23, Salon Owner, Nansana: Anita started braiding hair under a mango tree with no shop, just a mirror tied to the tree. 'My friends laughed at me. But I was the only hairdresser on that street.' She saved UGX 800,000 over 8 months from braiding profits and rented a small room. She taught herself new styles from YouTube. Her WhatsApp Status became her marketing — she posted every hairstyle she did. 'One post went viral in my area's WhatsApp group and I got 15 new customers in one week.' She now has 3 chairs, 2 employees, and sells hair products on the side. Monthly profit: UGX 1,500,000. Her advice: 'Never be ashamed of starting small. That mango tree was my MBA. Post your work everywhere — your phone is your best marketing tool.'

SUCCESS STORY — David Okello, 25, E-Commerce Reseller, Gulu: David started reselling refurbished phones and accessories on WhatsApp in 2024 with just UGX 300,000 in initial stock. 'I saw everyone in Gulu wanted smartphones but the shops were expensive. I found suppliers in Kampala who sold at wholesale prices and I became the bridge.' He learned product photography from YouTube — clean backgrounds, good lighting, multiple angles. 'The moment I started posting professional-looking photos on my WhatsApp Status, my sales doubled overnight. People trust what looks legit.' He expanded to a full online electronics shop with headphones, chargers, power banks, and laptop accessories. His secret weapon: customer reviews. 'I ask every happy customer to send a video testimonial. I post those on my Status and new customers see real people vouching for me.' Monthly revenue: UGX 8,000,000, profit margin 20-25%. He now ships to Lira, Arua, and Soroti through bus courier services. His advice: 'Product photography and customer reviews are your two best salespeople. Invest time in both. And always offer cash on delivery — it builds trust faster than any advert.'

SUCCESS STORY — Rebecca Nambi, 30, SACCO Co-founder, Jinja: Rebecca co-founded a women's Village Savings and Loan Association (VSLA) in her Jinja trading community in 2022 with just 15 women saving UGX 5,000 per week. 'We started with a metal box and three padlocks. Every Saturday, rain or shine, we sat under a tree and saved.' Within a year, the group had saved UGX 4.5 million and was lending to members at 5% per month interest. 'The discipline was the hardest part. Some women wanted to skip weeks. We were strict — if you miss three meetings without a good reason, you're out.' By 2024, they had grown to 45 members and registered as a formal SACCO with the Registrar of Cooperatives. They applied for and received UGX 30 million in Emyooga seed capital under the women entrepreneurs category. 'The Emyooga application was not easy — we needed audited books, a registered office, and proof of consistent savings. Many SACCOs fail at this stage because their records are messy.' Today, their SACCO has 150+ members, total savings of UGX 180 million, and lends out UGX 500 million annually at 2% per month. They've helped members start poultry farms, tailoring shops, and produce businesses across Jinja district. Her advice: 'Group discipline and record-keeping are everything. A SACCO is only as strong as its weakest record book. Invest in proper accounting software or at minimum a well-maintained ledger. And never lend more than 3x a member's savings — that's how SACCOs collapse.'

SUCCESS STORY — Kenneth Tumwebaze, 27, Carpenter & Furniture Maker, Fort Portal: Kenneth was a university dropout who returned to Fort Portal in 2023 with no money and no plan. 'My family was disappointed. Everyone expected me to get an office job.' He applied for the Youth Livelihood Programme (YLP) with a group of 8 friends and received UGX 12 million. He invested UGX 1.5 million in tools — a circular saw, electric planer, chisels, and hand tools — and rented a small workshop behind the Fort Portal taxi park for UGX 80,000/month. 'My first month, I made 3 beds and a wardrobe. I earned UGX 400,000 profit. Not much, but I could see the potential.' His turning point: he walked into every primary and secondary school in Fort Portal Tourism City and offered to make sample desks. 'Most schools were using broken furniture. I made one desk, delivered it free, and said — if you like it, I'll make 100 more.' Two schools placed orders for 200 desks at UGX 100,000 each — UGX 20 million in revenue. He then won a local government tender for office furniture worth UGX 40 million. He now employs 8 full-time workers and 4 apprentices. His workshop produces beds, wardrobes, school desks, doors, and fitted kitchens. Monthly revenue: UGX 6,000,000-10,000,000. He has fully repaid his YLP loan. His advice: 'Government contracts are the fastest path to scale for a carpenter. Schools, hospitals, local government offices — they all need furniture and they pay big orders at once. Walk in with a sample and a quotation. Most craftsmen wait for customers. I go find them.'
